source: Post-war reorganisation and development of education- memorandum by R. Henderson__CO273:678:10
filename: Post-war reorganisation and development of education- memorandum by R. Henderson__CO273:678:10.txt
summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** A memorandum titled *“Post-War Reorganisation and Development of Education in Malaya”* proposing a comprehensive redesign of Malaya’s education system after WWII, with strong emphasis on **vernacular schooling, practical/technical education, and links to economic reconstruction and social stability**.  
- **Geography/time period:** Focuses on **Malaya (including Singapore references)** in the **immediate post-war period (1945–1946)**; the author notes it was drafted during **internment** and revised later. Key correspondence dates include **1 Dec 1945**, **22 Jan 1946**, **29 Jan 1946**, **12 Feb 1946**.  
- **Key actors/organisations:** Author **R. Henderson** (also appears as P. Henderson in one letter—uncertain if OCR error); correspondence with the **Colonial Office (Under Secretary of State)** and an **Assistant Educational Adviser**; references to the **Advisory Committee on Education in the Colonies** (and a hoped-for Far East sub-committee), Malayan government departments (e.g., **Public Works Department**), and institutions such as **Raffles College**, **College of Medicine**, and the **Technical College (Kuala Lumpur)**.  
- **Major proposals (school system):** Calls for **equal educational opportunity irrespective of race/creed**, expansion and unification of **vernacular education**, and a structured system including **junior/senior vernacular schools**, **junior/senior English schools**, **technical schools/“technifactories” (school + factory)**, **technical high schools**, and **secondary (academic) schools**—aimed at reducing overproduction of “white-collar” job seekers and improving civic cohesion.  
- **Notable pre-war conditions & numbers:** States Malays generally received a **four-year** vernacular course; in Singapore **2,000–3,000 Malay children** reportedly lacked school places; estimates only **~50% of Chinese school-age population** received any education; claims **<20%** of pupils in English/aided schools came from English-speaking homes; pre-war technical schools numbered **five** (motor engineering dominant in four; cabinet-making in one).  
- **Technical/vocational development & reconstruction:** Urges rapid re-establishment and expansion of technical training aligned to labour-market needs, including caution that post-war conditions may create a **2–3 year surplus of motor mechanics**; proposes new trade groupings (building trades, electrical/air-conditioning, plumbing/sanitation, etc.) and exploration of new industries (e.g., **plastics**, **glassware**, **pottery**, fibre from **pineapple leaf waste**, and other materials).  
- **Crafts and rural economic support:** Proposes a centrally organised **“Malayan Craft Developments”** with a **Craft Research Institute** (suggested Kuala Lumpur, linked to the Technical College) to address under-capitalisation and marketing problems in crafts (example: **Trengganu/Kelantan weaving**, cited **~5,000 looms**; local market estimated **~500,000 yards/year**). Suggests a government **loan/float** of about **$5,000 rising to $50,000** as operations scale.  
- **Technical College reform & scale:** Argues for rebuilding/expanding the Technical College and changing its governance to a **grant-in-aid/independent model** like Raffles College; projects demand of **150–160 graduates/year** for government departments (plus up to **50/year** for private industry), implying **~800** engineering students on a **four-year** course; proposes teacher-training within the Technical College (about **100 male craft teachers/year** and **50 women/year**, four-year courses). Includes financial comparisons claiming substantial savings under a revised scholarship/fee structure (e.g., **$34,250** first-year saving; **$170,015** total saving over a four-year course—figures as stated in text).
